description:
A logistics firm must devise a procurement and storage schedule for a critical fuel additive over a 19‑week operational cycle.  
The starting stock at the cycle’s outset is 93 gallons.  
In each week t = 1,…,19, the supplier permits the firm to place a fuel‑additive order, the size of which must be between 9 and 70 gallons.  
Every order, however, requires a lead time of 1 week before it becomes available to meet requirements or replenish stock.  
Weekly additive requirements are as follows:  
* Week 1: 41 gallons  
* Week 2: 42 gallons  
* Week 3: 50 gallons  
* Week 4: 13 gallons  
* Week 5: 22 gallons  
* Week 6: 31 gallons  
* Week 7: 39 gallons  
* Week 8: 56 gallons  
* Week 9: 21 gallons  
* Week 10: 46 gallons  
* Week 11: 22 gallons  
* Week 12: 23 gallons  
* Week 13: 12 gallons  
* Week 14: 22 gallons  
* Week 15: 17 gallons  
* Week 16: 28 gallons  
* Week 17: 58 gallons  
* Week 18: 30 gallons  
* Week 19: 31 gallons  

Shortfalls are allowed, but any unmet requirement is lost and cannot be recovered later.  
Throughout the cycle, additive quantities may be treated as continuous, and the total volume held in the storage tank at any moment must not surpass the capacity limit of 93 gallons.  

In week 13, the maximum permissible order size is restricted to 60 gallons.  
In week 4, the on‑hand stock must be no less than 72 gallons.  

Total expenditure over the cycle comprises three elements: the procurement cost, charged at 2 per gallon ordered; the storage cost, assessed at 3 per gallon kept from one week to the next; and the shortage penalty, levied at 12 per gallon of unsatisfied requirement. The objective is to identify the optimal order quantity for each week and to monitor the ensuing stock and shortage levels, thereby minimizing the overall expenditure.

description:
A shipping company must devise a replenishment and storage schedule for a critical fuel reserve across a 5-week voyage.  
The initial fuel reserve at the start of the voyage is 26 thousand liters.  
In each week \(t = 1, \dots, 5\), the supplier permits the company to request a fuel delivery whose volume must be between 17 and 50 thousand liters.  
However, each delivery requested will require a 3‑week transit time before it becomes available for consumption or for adding to the reserves.  
The projected fuel consumption for each week is as follows:  
* Week 1: 32 thousand liters  
* Week 2: 37 thousand liters  
* Week 3: 30 thousand liters  
* Week 4: 49 thousand liters  
* Week 5: 50 thousand liters  

Shortfalls are allowed, but any unsatisfied consumption is simply lost and will not be carried forward.  
Throughout the voyage, fuel quantities may be treated as continuous, and the total volume of fuel held in reserve tanks at any moment must not exceed the tank capacity of 57 thousand liters.  

In week 5, the maximum delivery volume allowed is 17 thousand liters.  
In week 1, the on‑hand reserve must be at least 46 thousand liters.  

The total voyage cost comprises three elements: the delivery cost, which is 5 per thousand liters ordered; the storage cost, which is 2 per thousand liters kept from one week to the next; and the shortage penalty, which is 6 per thousand liters of unmet consumption. Determine the optimal delivery volume for each week and monitor the resulting reserve levels and shortfalls so as to minimize the total cost.

The initial fuel reserve in the main depot at the voyage's commencement is 11 kiloliters.  
For each leg \( t = 1, \dots, 5 \), the fuel supplier permits the company to schedule a delivery, the volume of which must be between 19 and 71 kiloliters.  
Nevertheless, any scheduled delivery experiences a lag of 3 days before it reaches the depot and becomes available for consumption or to augment reserves.  
The projected fuel consumption for each leg is as follows:  
* Leg 1: consumption 48 kiloliters  
* Leg 2: consumption 45 kiloliters  
* Leg 3: consumption 47 kiloliters  
* Leg 4: consumption 28 kiloliters  
* Leg 5: consumption 38 kiloliters  

Fuel deficits are tolerated, but any consumption not met from available reserves is simply lost and will not be compensated later.  
Throughout the voyage, fuel quantities may be treated as continuous, and the total volume of fuel physically held in the depot at any instant must not surpass the storage limit of 42 kiloliters.  

Specifically on leg 2, the maximum permissible delivery volume is 71 kiloliters.  
Also on leg 2, the on-site fuel reserve must be maintained at a minimum of 2 kiloliters.  

The aggregate expenditure over the voyage comprises three elements: the procurement cost, amounting to 3 per kiloliter ordered; the storage cost, amounting to 3 per kiloliter of fuel kept from one leg to the next; and the deficit penalty, amounting to 9 per kiloliter of unmet consumption. The objective is to ascertain the optimal delivery volume for each leg and to monitor the consequent reserve and deficit levels so as to minimize the total expenditure.

description:
A shipping company must devise a fuel procurement and storage strategy for a fleet of vessels over a voyage lasting 5 consecutive legs.  
The initial fuel reserve in the main depot at the voyage's commencement is 11 kiloliters.  
For each leg \( t = 1, \dots, 5 \), the fuel supplier permits the company to schedule a delivery, the volume of which must be between 19 and 71 kiloliters.  
Nevertheless, any scheduled delivery experiences a lag of 3 days before it reaches the depot and becomes available for consumption or to augment reserves.  
The projected fuel consumption for each leg is as follows:  
* Leg 1: consumption 48 kiloliters  
* Leg 2: consumption 45 kiloliters  
* Leg 3: consumption 47 kiloliters  
* Leg 4: consumption 28 kiloliters  
* Leg 5: consumption 38 kiloliters  

Fuel deficits are tolerated, but any consumption not met from available reserves is simply lost and will not be compensated later.  
Throughout the voyage, fuel quantities may be treated as continuous, and the total volume of fuel physically held in the depot at any instant must not surpass the storage limit of 42 kiloliters.  

Specifically on leg 2, the maximum permissible delivery volume is 71 kiloliters.  
Also on leg 2, the on-site fuel reserve must be maintained at a minimum of 2 kiloliters.  

The aggregate expenditure over the voyage comprises three elements: the procurement cost, amounting to 3 per kiloliter ordered; the storage cost, amounting to 3 per kiloliter of fuel kept from one leg to the next; and the deficit penalty, amounting to 9 per kiloliter of unmet consumption. The objective is to ascertain the optimal delivery volume for each leg and to monitor the consequent reserve and deficit levels so as to minimize the total expenditure.

description:
A shipping company must devise a fuel procurement and storage strategy for a fleet of vessels over a voyage lasting 5 consecutive legs.  
The initial fuel reserve in the main depot at the voyage's commencement is 11 kiloliters.  
For each leg \( t = 1, \dots, 5 \), the fuel supplier permits the company to schedule a delivery, the volume of which must be between 19 and 71 kiloliters.  
Nevertheless, any scheduled delivery experiences a lag of 3 days before it reaches the depot and becomes available for consumption or to augment reserves.  
The projected fuel consumption for each leg is as follows:  
* Leg 1: consumption 48 kiloliters  
* Leg 2: consumption 45 kiloliters  
* Leg 3: consumption 47 kiloliters  
* Leg 4: consumption 28 kiloliters  
* Leg 5: consumption 38 kiloliters  

Fuel deficits are tolerated, but any consumption not met from available reserves is simply lost and will not be compensated later.  
Throughout the voyage, fuel quantities may be treated as continuous, and the total volume of fuel physically held in the depot at any instant must not surpass the storage limit of 42 kiloliters.  

Specifically on leg 2, the maximum permissible delivery volume is 71 kiloliters.  
Also on leg 2, the on-site fuel reserve must be maintained at a minimum of 2 kiloliters.  

The aggregate expenditure over the voyage comprises three elements: the procurement cost, amounting to 3 per kiloliter ordered; the storage cost, amounting to 3 per kiloliter of fuel kept from one leg to the next; and the deficit penalty, amounting to 9 per kiloliter of unmet consumption. The objective is to ascertain the optimal delivery volume for each leg and to monitor the consequent reserve and deficit levels so as to minimize the total expenditure.

description:
A retail distribution center must devise a procurement and storage strategy for a seasonal product across a 14-day promotional window.  
Initial stock at the start of the window is 55 items.  
In each day t = 1, …, 14, the wholesaler permits an order whose size must be between 19 and 22 items.  
Every order placed experiences a lead time of 1 day before it becomes available to meet customer requirements or replenish stock.  
Daily customer requirements for the product are as follows:  
* Day 1: 60 items  
* Day 2: 21 items  
* Day 3: 41 items  
* Day 4: 52 items  
* Day 5: 19 items  
* Day 6: 49 items  
* Day 7: 47 items  
* Day 8: 42 items  
* Day 9: 36 items  
* Day 10: 47 items  
* Day 11: 50 items  
* Day 12: 24 items  
* Day 13: 10 items  
* Day 14: 13 items  

Stockouts are allowed, but any unsatisfied demand is lost and cannot be recovered later.  
Throughout the 14 days, product quantities may be fractional, and the total stock physically present in the warehouse at any moment must not surpass the storage limit of 55 items.  

Specifically, on day 11, the maximum allowable order size is 20 items.  
On day 12, the physical stock on hand must be no less than 3 items.  

Total cost comprises three elements: the procurement cost, equal to 5 per item ordered; the storage cost, equal to 1 per item held overnight; and the lost‑sales penalty, equal to 12 per item of unmet demand.  
Determine the optimal order size for each day and the corresponding stock and shortage amounts so as to minimize the total expenditure.

description:
A hospital must devise a procurement and blood‑plasma storage strategy for a critical therapeutic agent over an 8‑week planning cycle.  
The initial stock at the start of the planning window is 92 liters.  
In each week \(t = 1,\dots,8\), the supplier permits the hospital to request a shipment whose volume must be between 8 and 25 liters.  
Every shipment, however, requires a lead time of 2 weeks before it becomes available for patient treatment or to replenish the storage.  
The weekly consumption of the agent is as follows:  
* Week 1: 60 liters  
* Week 2: 38 liters  
* Week 3: 37 liters  
* Week 4: 53 liters  
* Week 5: 36 liters  
* Week 6: 11 liters  
* Week 7: 41 liters  
* Week 8: 30 liters  

Supply shortfalls are tolerated, but any unsatisfied consumption is lost and cannot be deferred to later weeks.  
Throughout the 8‑week horizon, agent quantities may be treated as continuous, and the total volume kept in the refrigerated storage at any moment must not exceed the capacity limit of 92 liters.  

In week 6, the maximum allowable shipment volume is restricted to 19 liters.  
In week 3, the on‑hand stock must be maintained at a level of at least 1 liter.  

The overall cost comprises three elements: the procurement cost, charged at 1 per liter ordered; the storage cost, charged at 1 per liter carried over from one week to the next; and a shortage penalty, levied at 11 per liter of unmet consumption.  
The objective is to decide the optimal shipment volume for each week and to monitor the resulting stock and shortage levels so as to minimize the total expenditure.

description:
A logistics center must devise a procurement and storage strategy for a critical fuel additive over an 11-week operational window.  
The initial stock at the start of the planning interval is 58 gallons.  
In each week \( t = 1, \dots, 11 \), the supplier permits an order whose volume must be between 19 and 35 gallons.  
Nevertheless, every order placed is subject to a 3‑week lead time before it becomes available to meet consumption or replenish stock.  
The weekly consumption of the additive is as follows:  
* Week 1: 50 gallons  
* Week 2: 47 gallons  
* Week 3: 21 gallons  
* Week 4: 51 gallons  
* Week 5: 42 gallons  
* Week 6: 12 gallons  
* Week 7: 58 gallons  
* Week 8: 30 gallons  
* Week 9: 14 gallons  
* Week 10: 22 gallons  
* Week 11: 51 gallons  

Stock‑outs are tolerated, but any unsatisfied consumption is lost and cannot be recovered later.  
Throughout the planning horizon, additive quantities may be treated as continuous, and the total physical inventory held at any moment must not surpass the tank capacity of 58 gallons.  

In week 3, the maximum allowable order volume is 34 gallons.  
By week 9, the physical stock on hand must be no less than 3 gallons.  

The aggregate expense over the 11 weeks comprises three elements: the procurement cost, amounting to 5 per gallon ordered; the storage cost, equal to 1 per gallon carried from one week to the next; and the shortage penalty, assessed at 11 per gallon of unmet consumption.  
The objective is to identify the optimal order volume for each week and to monitor the resulting inventory and shortage levels so as to minimize total expenditure.
